review_summary: This unit provides enough evidence for concept checks on patch-based spatial pretext tasks and masked reconstruction. The questions focus on the learning signal rather than paper-specific details.

question_bank.question: In relative patch prediction with a 3x3 grid, what is the model asked to predict?
choices: Which of the eight surrounding positions a patch occupies relative to the center patch.
The exact RGB values of every pixel in the center patch.
The object class label for the entire image.
A continuous camera angle for the image.
question_bank.explanation: The center patch acts as a reference. The task turns spatial reasoning into classification over the possible relative locations of another patch.

transcript_quotes: with respect to the center patch

question_bank.question: Why might a jigsaw pretext task use a fixed lookup table of 64 permutations instead of all 9! permutations?
choices: The full permutation space is too large, so restricting it makes the classification task tractable.
The image contains only 64 pixels, so no more classes are possible.
The method requires exactly one class per object category.
Using all permutations would remove the need for a neural network.
question_bank.explanation: A 3x3 jigsaw has an enormous number of possible arrangements. Selecting a manageable set of permutations turns the task into a practical multiclass prediction problem.

question_bank.question: What is the central learning signal in image inpainting as a pretext task?
choices: Predict the masked image region from the visible context and compare it with the original pixels.
Predict the image class using only manually annotated category labels.
Sort images by their capture time without changing any pixels.
Choose the nearest image in pixel space and copy its class.
question_bank.explanation: Inpainting hides part of the input and trains the model to reconstruct the missing region. The original image provides the target, so the model learns from context without external labels.
